title: Mornings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: Six firms agree to cover their own grid upgrades under Whitmer plan while local fights over noise, water and bills continue.

lines.text: Good morning and welcome to Mornings with Graham & Emmy — the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy. It's Friday, July 17, and we're kicking off the morning edition with some fresh movement on who pays for all this growth.
That's right, Emmy. Today Governor Whitmer released a voluntary agreement asking data center operators to pay for the grid upgrades their projects trigger, and six companies have already signed on.
Six out of the gate? That's notable. What's the bigger picture here — why does this pledge even exist, and what does it change for regular ratepayers?
It comes as multiple large projects are seeking approvals across the state. The idea is to shift some of that cost burden away from Michigan families who didn't ask for these campuses.
So historically, how have these costs been handled? Have utilities just passed upgrades straight to bills before?

That's been the concern in several communities. When a hyperscale project lands, the transmission lines and substations needed can run into the hundreds of millions, and without agreements like this, ratepayers have shouldered a big chunk.
And water is another flashpoint we've heard about. How does power tie into water strain in these fights?

Data centers use a lot of both. Cooling systems pull serious water, and the power plants that feed them often do too. One recent denial involved a request that would have supplied a campus needing power for two million homes.
Wow. So if a utility says no, what happens next for that company and the towns around it?
They could appeal, look for alternative sites, or scale back. Either way, it highlights how local resources are already pushing back against the pace of these proposals.
And farmland and small towns — what's at stake there beyond the utility lines?

Noise, traffic, property values, and just the character of places like Lyon Township or Dowagiac. Residents are already suing over nonstop hum from existing sites and organizing against rezonings that could bring more in.
We saw that in Taylor too — neighbors fighting a rezoning near homes. How does the Whitmer pledge address those quality-of-life issues, or does it mostly focus on power?

It zeroes in on power and grid costs. The noise and traffic fights are still playing out at the township level, with boards set to vote and some places like Pere Marquette advancing moratoriums to buy time for rules.
Pere Marquette is pausing new proposals while they draft permanent regulations. That seems like a direct response to the rush. How many other townships are watching this?

Quite a few. The pledge is now circulating among communities facing their own fights, and local leaders are split — some call it a good first step, others say it needs binding rules to really protect residents.
Meanwhile Democrats in the legislature have a package that would mandate community agreements and long-term energy contracts. How does that fit with the voluntary pledge?

It's a parallel track. The bills would require data centers to meet standards on power, water and workforce so residents aren't subsidizing growth through higher bills. The pledge is voluntary; the legislation aims to make pieces of it mandatory.
And we've got an OpenAI project in Saline that just got a scaled-back tax break. Does that show companies are willing to negotiate on incentives too?

It does. They reduced the ask, and the township approved a smaller package — still forgone revenue for roads and schools, but at about one-ninth the original level. It sets a precedent for how much locals contribute.
So pulling it together, Graham, this pledge lands right as lawsuits, noise complaints, water denials and moratoriums are all active. What happens next — more sign-ons or push for the bills?
Both tracks are moving. We'll see if more firms join the voluntary agreement and how the legislative package advances. Local votes in places like Lyon and Taylor will also shape where projects land.
And for ratepayers specifically, if these pledges stick, we could avoid some of those bill spikes we've been warned about.
Exactly. That's the core promise — companies covering the upgrades they trigger instead of spreading it across everyone else's monthly statement.
Alright, let's move to quick hits. Yesterday Lyon Township neighbors blasted a project's noise and traffic ahead of the final vote. What are they saying?

Residents near the proposed site say the constant hum and truck traffic will wreck their quality of life. The township board decides soon, and even families 30 minutes away in Oakland County could feel power and water strain if it goes through.
And in Dowagiac, neighbors on Louise Ave. are suing over nonstop noise from a hyperscale facility. Class-action moving forward?
Yes, Cass County locals want relief from what they call an unbearable intrusion. The suit is advancing to fight the constant hum.
Taylor residents are organizing against a rezoning that could open the door to data centers near homes. Concerns there?
Noise, traffic and power demand. The proposal would change land-use rules, and pushback could force the city to add stricter conditions or reconsider.
Pere Marquette Township advanced a moratorium to halt proposals while they write permanent rules. Why the pause?
To give residents time to weigh impacts on roads, power lines and property values. Mason County officials are watching as other townships consider similar steps.
Whitmer's pledge is splitting local leaders — some like the first step, others want binding rules. How's that playing out?
The proposal is circulating among townships in their own fights. It's voluntary, so the debate is whether it goes far enough without mandates.
OpenAI got a reduced tax break in Saline after scaling back. What does that mean for Washtenaw County residents?
Still some forgone revenue for roads, schools and emergency services, but at roughly one-ninth the level first proposed. Sets terms for local contribution.
And the Democratic package of bills would require community agreements and long-term energy contracts. Goal there?
Prevent residents from subsidizing growth through higher utility bills and water strain. It would reshape how future campuses get sited.
On deck for listeners — any meetings they can actually attend this week or next?

Nothing major scheduled today on the public calendar, but township boards in Lyon and Taylor have decisions coming up soon. Check the Michigan Data Center Tracker for exact dates and agendas as they post.
Always good to stay on top of those local votes. Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker.
We're back tomorrow. Thanks for listening.

episode_title: Whitmer's Power Pledge Draws Six Signers as Data Center Cost Battles Intensify
